_source: Christopher Vogler 'The Writer's Journey', Joseph Campbell 'Hero with a Thousand Faces', TV Tropes
_note: Long-tail narrative data. Deep archetype descriptions with cross-media examples and subversions.

hero.description: Character who grows through adversity; audience identification vessel

subtypes.reluctant_hero: Refuses the call initially; dragged into adventure; more relatable (Frodo, Jon Snow)
subtypes.anti_hero: Does right for wrong reasons or wrong for right reasons; morally complex (Walter White, Deadpool)
subtypes.tragic_hero: Noble character destroyed by fatal flaw (hamartia); Shakespearean (Macbeth, Anakin Skywalker)
subtypes.everyman_hero: No special powers; audience surrogate; strength through determination (John McClane)

hero.shadow: The hero's dark potential; what they could become without growth

mentor.description: Wise guide who prepares hero for journey then steps back
attributes: Often sacrifices themselves at critical moment
mentor.function: Represents hero's higher self; what they could become
mentor.subversion: Mentor who is wrong or limited; hero must surpass them (Rocky's Mickey, Whiplash's Fletcher)

shadow_villain.description: Antagonist representing the dark side of the hero's journey
shadow_villain.best_villains: Have understandable motivation; audience can see their point (Thanos, Magneto, Erik Killmonger)

types.ideological: Believes their cause justifies means (Killmonger, Ra's al Ghul)
types.trauma_driven: Origin wound that hero must address empathetically (Darth Vader, Loki)
types.pure_chaos: Rare; works when contrast with order is the point (Joker in The Dark Knight)

trickster.description: Agent of change through chaos and humor; disrupts status quo
trickster.function: Comic relief + reveals truth through jokes + challenges assumptions
trickster.importance: Prevents story from taking itself too seriously; humanizes drama

shapeshifter.description: Character whose allegiance or nature is ambiguous; creates uncertainty
shapeshifter.function: Keeps audience off-balance; can be ally or enemy
shapeshifter.writing_tip: Best when final alignment is surprising but inevitable in retrospect

herald.description: Announces change; delivers the call to adventure
herald.function: Catalyst for plot; brings news that disrupts hero's ordinary world

threshold_guardian.description: Tests hero's readiness before each new world/stage
threshold_guardian.function: Filters out unprepared; often not truly an enemy

heros_journey_12_stages.1_ordinary_world: Show hero's normal life and deficiency
heros_journey_12_stages.2_call_to_adventure: Disruption of ordinary world; new problem or opportunity
heros_journey_12_stages.5_crossing_threshold: Commits to adventure; point of no return
heros_journey_12_stages.6_tests_allies_enemies: Learn the rules of new world; form relationships
heros_journey_12_stages.7_approach_to_inmost_cave: Preparation for major challenge; raise stakes
heros_journey_12_stages.8_ordeal: Hero's lowest point; death/rebirth moment; transformation begins
heros_journey_12_stages.10_road_back: Return journey; antagonist may be resurgent
heros_journey_12_stages.11_resurrection: Final climax; hero is truly transformed; all they've learned tested
heros_journey_12_stages.12_return_with_elixir: Brings transformation back to ordinary world

beats: All is lost moment (second turning point)

seven_point_structure.hook: Start in interesting place; who is hero now (before change)
seven_point_structure.plot_turn_1: Something forces hero toward the midpoint
seven_point_structure.midpoint: Hero commits; turns from reactive to proactive
seven_point_structure.plot_turn_2: Hero discovers what they need to win

save_the_cat.overview: Blake Snyder's screenwriting structure; 15 beats mapped to page numbers

key_beats.opening_image: Visual statement of theme; before state
key_beats.theme_stated: Someone tells hero what story is REALLY about (usually ignored)
key_beats.catalyst: Page 12; inciting incident; what changes everything
key_beats.break_into_2: Hero enters new world; act 1→2 transition
key_beats.midpoint: Page 55; false victory or false defeat; stakes raised
key_beats.break_into_3: A-story and B-story combine; hero has what they need
key_beats.finale: Hero synthesizes lessons and defeats the antagonist

nonlinear_structures.in_medias_res: Start in middle of action; fill in backstory gradually
nonlinear_structures.parallel_storylines: Multiple POVs converging; reveal connections late (Babel, Crash)
nonlinear_structures.unreliable_narrator: Protagonist's account cannot be trusted; revelation reframes everything (Fight Club, Gone Girl)
nonlinear_structures.reverse_chronology: Begin at end; work backward; audience pieces together how we arrived (Memento)

dialogue_principles.subtext: Characters rarely say what they mean; reveal through what they DON'T say
dialogue_principles.conflict_in_every_scene: Every scene should have at least micro-conflict; agreement = boring scene
dialogue_principles.voice_differentiation: Each character should sound distinct without explanation
dialogue_principles.exposition_rule: Never have characters explain things they both already know ('As you know, Bob...')

theme_development.theme_vs_premise: Theme = central idea (love conquers fear). Premise = setup (orphan discovers magic).
theme_development.theme_in_opposition: Best themes are argued through characters who represent opposite sides
theme_development.show_dont_tell: Character actions that embody theme > characters saying theme aloud
theme_development.symbolism: Recurring visual/object/word that accumulates meaning; gun on wall in act 1 fires in act 3 (Chekhov)
